perturbation.original_text: 2. Non-Disclosure Of Confidential Information, SSI, Or PCII. Confidential Information, SSI, and PCII shall be held and treated in the strictest confidence in a manner designed to prevent the unauthorized disclosure to third parties. Confidential Information, SSI, and PCII may appear in records in written or electronic form, including but not limited to, drawings, photographs, schematics, plans, memorandums, reports, emails, videos, tape recordings, and Powerpoint presentations. Confidential Information, SSI, and/or PCII shall not be disclosed to third parties without the express written permission of the Executive Director of the TJPA or her designee; and if such permission is granted, then said information will be provided only upon the execution of a Non- Disclosure Agreement by that third party. If at any time Confidential Information, SSI, or PCII is disclosed in violation of this Agreement, including but not limited to any loss, theft, misuse, misplacement, unauthorized disclosure, or other security violation that the Designated Individual has knowledge of (whether or not he/she is personally involved), the Designated Individual shall immediately give the TJPA written notice of that fact and a detailed account of the circumstances regarding such disclosure. The Designated Individual’s anonymity shall be kept to the extent possible when reporting security violations.
perturbation.changed_text: 2. Non-Disclosure Of Confidential Information, SSI, Or PCII. Confidential Information, SSI, and PCII shall be held and treated with reasonable care. Confidential Information, SSI, and PCII may appear in records in written or electronic form, including but not limited to, drawings, photographs, schematics, plans, memorandums, reports, emails, videos, tape recordings, and Powerpoint presentations. Confidential Information, SSI, and/or PCII shall not be disclosed to third parties unless required by law. If at any time Confidential Information, SSI, or PCII is disclosed in violation of this Agreement, the Designated Individual shall notify the TJPA.
perturbation.explanation: This change weakens the non-disclosure obligation and removes the requirement for express written permission from the TJPA for disclosure. It also removes the requirement of a detailed account of circumstances if there is unauthorized disclosure. Finally, it removes the assurance of anonymity when reporting security violations.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts Cal. Gov. Code § 6254 because it weakens the protection of sensitive information held by the TJPA, potentially allowing for broader disclosure than permitted under the exemptions outlined in the California Public Records Act. The original text ensured stricter control over confidential information, aligning with the intent of the Act to protect certain types of information from public access. The modified text removes those strict controls.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, GOV 6254 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the non-disclosure obligation by replacing 'strictest confidence' with 'reasonable care', removing the requirement for express written permission from the TJPA for disclosure, and stating that disclosure is allowed if 'required by law'. It further removes the requirement for a detailed account of circumstances if there is unauthorized disclosure, and removes assurance of anonymity when reporting security violations. According to the Law Explanation, 'the modified text contradicts Cal. Gov. Code § 6254 because it weakens the protection of sensitive information held by the TJPA'. This aligns with the definition of 'Omission Legal Contradiction', which states that a legal contradiction in this category happens when a contract omits a legally mandated consumer protection, making it non-compliant.

perturbation.original_text: 6. Retention Limitations; Return Of Information. Upon the earlier occurrence of: (1) the TJPA’s written request; (2) ten (10) business days after Final Completion of the Project (or for non -successful Bidders, within ten (10) business days after the award of the Trade Work subcontract); (3) upon th e conclusion of the Designated Individual’s duties, association, or support to the Contractor in connection with the Project; or (4) upon the determination that the Designated Individual’s official duties do not require further access to Confidential Infor mation, SSI, or PCII, any and all documents containing Confidential Information, SSI, or PCII in the possession of the Designated Individual shall be promptly destroyed , or, if requested by the TJPA, returned to the TJPA . Upon returning or destroying the a forementioned documents, the Designated Individual shall sign the Affidavit of Return or Destruction in the form set forth in Section 01 35 70/AT2.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. Retention Limitations; Return Of Information. Upon the conclusion of the Designated Individual’s duties, association, or support to the Contractor in connection with the Project, documents containing Confidential Information, SSI, or PCII in the possession of the Designated Individual shall be retained until no longer needed.  Upon returning or destroying the aforementioned documents, the Designated Individual is encouraged to notify the TJPA.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the specific deadlines for returning or destroying confidential information and replaces it with a vague "until no longer needed" timeframe. It also eliminates the mandatory Affidavit of Return or Destruction. This introduces legal risk by potentially violating document retention requirements related to public projects.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts Cal. Gov. Code § 14755, which mandates specific document retention policies for state agencies and projects involving public funds. By removing the strict return or destruction deadlines and the requirement for an affidavit, the modified text creates a loophole that could lead to non-compliance with the state's mandated retention periods for project-related documents.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, GOV 14755 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts Cal. Gov. Code § 14755 by removing the strict return or destruction deadlines for confidential information and replacing it with a vague timeframe. The original text mandated specific actions upon defined events (TJPA's request, completion of the project, etc.), whereas the changed text allows indefinite retention 'until no longer needed.' This omission creates a loophole for non-compliance with the state's mandated retention periods, undermining the legal obligation to manage sensitive information responsibly, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. The Perturbation Explanation supports this by stating the elimination of deadlines and the affidavit introduces a risk of violating document retention requirements related to public projects. Scraped Law Snippet 1 confirms the existence of California Code, GOV 14755, establishing document retention policies for state agencies.

perturbation.original_text: 7. Violations And Remedies. Unauthorized Disclosure: If a Contractor or any Designated Individual knows or has a reasonable belief that documents subject to the Specification have been or may be disclosed to an unauthorized person(s), the Contractor and/or Designated Individual shall imme diately notify the TJPA. The TJPA shall make the final determination as to whether a violation of the Specification occurred and the course of action to address the violation. Remedies: Any Contractor or Designated Individual that violates the Specificat ion with respect to Confidential Information, SSI and/or PCII shall, at the TJPA’s election, be subject to any of the following: (1) written reprimand of the Contractor and/or Designated Individual; (2) termination of the Contractor or Designated Individual’s access to Confidential Information, SSI, or PCII; (3) directive to the Contractor to immediately suspended the Designated Individual from performing any further work relating to the Project; (4) if a contract has been awarded, treatment of the unautho rized disclosure of Confidential Information, SSI, or PCII as a material breach of the contract, warranting the termination of the contract for cause; (5) commencement of legal proceedings for any other legal rights and remedies, including injunctive relie f and monetary damages arising from the breach of contract; (6) reporting of any violation related to SSI or PCII to the Department of Homeland Security, the Transportation Security Administration, or the Department of Transportation (these agencies may also independently assess penalties and/or take corrective action as provided under Federal law, including 49 C.F.R. §§ 15.17 and 1520.17); (7) disqualification of a Contractor from bidding on any work for the Project; and/or (8) written directive to retriev e all Confidential Information, SSI, and/or PCII disclosed to unauthorized persons at Contractor’s expense and to cease future unauthorized disclosure. If legal proceedings to enforce any remedy set forth under this paragraph are commenced by the TJPA, the prevailing party in such legal action shall be entitled to reasonable attorney’s fees and costs . Furthermore, Contractor agrees to indemnify the TJPA for any expenses or damages (including attorney’s fees or costs) incurred by the TJPA arising out of or relating to the Contractor and/or Designated Individual’s unauthorized disclosure of Confidential Information, SSI, and/or PCII.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. Violations And Remedies. Unauthorized Disclosure: If a Contractor or any Designated Individual knows or has a reasonable belief that documents subject to the Specification have been or may be disclosed to an unauthorized person(s), the Contractor and/or Designated Individual should consider notifying the TJPA. Remedies: Any Contractor or Designated Individual that violates the Specification with respect to Confidential Information, SSI and/or PCII may, at the TJPA’s election, be subject to a written reprimand. Furthermore, Contractor agrees to indemnify the TJPA for direct damages arising out of the Contractor and/or Designated Individual’s unauthorized disclosure of Confidential Information, SSI, and/or PCII.
perturbation.explanation: This change significantly weakens the enforcement provisions. It changes the mandatory notification of unauthorized disclosure to a suggestion, and drastically reduces the potential remedies available to the TJPA. Removing the reporting requirements to federal agencies also introduces a compliance risk.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts 49 CFR § 1520.13(a), which mandates specific reporting procedures for security incidents involving Sensitive Security Information (SSI). By removing the requirement to report violations to federal agencies like the Department of Homeland Security and limiting the remedies available to the TJPA, the modified text creates a legal contradiction and increases the risk of non-compliance with federal security regulations.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 49 CFR § 1520.13 - Marking SSI. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 49 CFR § 1520.13 - Marking SSI. CFR prev | next § 1520.13 Marking SSI . (a) Marking of paper records. In the case of paper records containing SSI , a covered person must mark the record by placing the protective marking conspicuously on the top, and the distribution limitation statement on the bottom, of— (1) The outside of any front and back cover, including a binder cover or folder, if the document has a front and back cover; (2) Any title page; and (3) Each page of the document. (b) Protective marking. The protective marking is: SENSITIVE SECURITY INFORMATION . (c) Distribution limitation statement. The distribution limitation statement is: WARNING: This record contains Sensitive Security Information that is controlled under 49 CFR parts 15 and 1520 . No part of this record may be disclosed to persons without a “need to know”, as defined in 49 CFR parts 15 and 1520 , except with the written permission of the Administrator of the Transportation Security Administration or the Secretary of Transportation. Unauthorized release may result in civil penalty or other action. For U.S. government agencies, public disclosure is governed by 5 U.S.C. 552 and 49 CFR parts 15 and 1520 . (d) Other types of records. In the case of non-paper records that contain SSI , including motion picture films, videotape recordings, audio recording, and electronic and magnetic records, a covered person must clearly and conspicuously mark the records with the protective marking and the distribution limitation statement such that the viewer or listener is reasonably likely to see or hear them when obtaining access to the contents of the record . perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts 49 CFR § 1520.13 because it changes the mandatory reporting of security incidents involving Sensitive Security Information (SSI) to a discretionary suggestion. Specifically, the original text states the Contractor 'shall immediately notify the TJPA,' whereas the modified text states they 'should consider notifying the TJPA.' Furthermore, the removal of the requirement to report violations to federal agencies, as stated in the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation, directly conflicts with the obligations related to SSI as outlined in 49 CFR § 1520.13, which concerns marking and handling SSI to protect it from unauthorized disclosure.

perturbation.original_text: 10. Governing Law. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California, without regard to conflict of laws principles. The Designated Individual specifically and irrevocably consents to the exclusive jurisdiction of any Federal or State court in the County of San Francisco and State of California with respect to all matters concerning this Agreement and its enforcement.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. Governing Law. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of another state. The Designated Individual consents to the jurisdiction of any court with jurisdiction over the Contractor.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the governing law to another state, the agreement may no longer comply with California specific statutes. By removing the specific agreement to jurisdiction and venue in California, it makes it much harder for the TJPA to sue for breach of contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 394, which establishes specific venue rules for actions brought by or against a local government entity. The original clause specifying jurisdiction in San Francisco County aligns with the intent of this statute to ensure a fair venue for disputes involving the TJPA. By removing this specific provision and vaguely referring to 'any court with jurisdiction', the modified text could potentially allow the Contractor to force litigation in a venue far from the project site, hindering the TJPA's ability to effectively litigate and potentially increasing costs.

OF THE PLACE OF TRIAL, RECLASSIFICATION, AND COORDINATION OF CIVIL ACTIONS [392 - 404.9] ( Heading of Title 4 amended by Stats. 1999, Ch. 344, Sec. 6. ) CHAPTER 1. Place of Trial [392 - 403] ( Heading of Chapter 1 amended by Stats. 1999, Ch. 344, Sec. 7. ) 394. (a) An action or proceeding against a county, or city and county, a city, or local agency, may be tried in the county, or city and county, or the county in which the city or local agency is situated, unless the action or proceeding is brought by a county, or city and county, a city, or local agency, in which case it may be tried in any county, or city and county, not a party thereto and in which the city or local agency is not situated. Except for actions initiated by the local child support agency pursuant to Section 17400, 17402, 17404, or 17416 of the Family Code, any action or proceeding brought by a county, city and county, city, or local agency within a certain county, or city and county, against a resident of another county, city and county, or city, or a corporation doing business in the latter, shall be, on motion of either party, transferred for trial to a county, or city and county, other than t
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 394. The original text specified jurisdiction in San Francisco County, aligning with the statute's intent to ensure a fair venue for disputes involving the TJPA, which would be considered a 'local agency' within a certain county, as stated in the provided snippet. The modified text removes this specific provision and vaguely refers to 'any court with jurisdiction,' allowing the contractor to potentially force litigation in a venue far from the project site, hindering the TJPA's ability to effectively litigate and potentially increasing costs, contradicting the purpose of CCP 394.
